critic_raw:
Verdict review — radiology AI triage:

Core finding: the comparison protocol creates an asymmetry that undermines the superhuman performance claim. Specifically: (a) human readers were not shown prior CT exams, which are required for growth-rate assessment — the model's primary input; and (b) three of four readers were general radiologists rather than thoracic specialists, depressing the human baseline below the clinical standard for screening reads.

The model's absolute performance (0.91/0.80 sensitivity/specificity on N=2,300) is credible and meaningful as a standalone result. The problem is the comparative framing: calling the result 'superhuman' requires a fair competition, and the current protocol did not provide one.

Ancillary observations: triage-assistance deployment scope is appropriate; inference time is acceptable; screening-program-only data limits generalizability but does not invalidate the core use case.

Verdict: empirical_test_agreed
A reader study with prior exam access and a specialist-majority panel would resolve whether the superiority is genuine.
adjudication_raw: Single-agent baseline, run 3. All three must-find issues identified. No must-not claims raised. Inference time and specificity matching correctly assessed as non-issues. Verdict: empirical_test_agreed.

empirical_test.measure: sensitivity at matched specificity in equitable reader study
empirical_test.success_criterion: model sensitivity remains higher than specialist-majority panel mean when readers have prior exam access
empirical_test.failure_criterion: specialist readers with prior access match or exceed model sensitivity, attributing the original gap to the protocol asymmetry
